title: Śrī Guru-vandanā (Muni-vandanā) Sūtra
summary: This is a devotional recitation in which the practitioner bows to all true Jain monks (munis) living anywhere in the inhabited world, and then honors, name by name, the unbroken chain of teachers (the paṭṭāvalī, or lineage of pontiffs) of the Anchalgachchha — also called the Vidhipakṣa Gaccha. It begins with Mahāvīra's chief disciple Sudharmāsvāmī, runs through the great early teachers, reaches Dādā Śrī Āryarakṣitasūri (counted the 47th in the succession), continues down to the recent Sāgara-named ācāryas, and closes by saluting, with 108 three-fold prostrations, the ācārya named Duḥṣpasaha who tradition holds will be the very last of this descending age. It is recited so that the worshipper renews reverence for living renunciants and gratefully roots their own practice in this teacher-to-disciple lineage.
ritualPlacement: A sajjhāy (svādhyāya, sacred-study recitation), not one of the six Āvaśyakas. In the Daivasika (Devasi) pratikramaṇa it falls in the closing study-portion: after the Laghu Atiyār (the short confession, 4th Āvaśyaka) and before the Drumapuṣpikā / Pañca-Parameṣṭhi / Mannaha Jiṇāṇaṁ sajjhāy cluster, leading toward the conclusion of the sāmāyika. The book assigns it specifically to Devasi; unlike the core Āvaśyaka sūtras it is not part of the shared pakkhī/cāumāsī/sāṁvatsarī substitution chain, so the standard 'replace the word pakkhī with cāumāsī/saṁvatsarī' note does not apply here.
authorAndSource: Composite. The opening 'Jāvanta Kevi Sāhū' gāthā (vv.1–2) is a traditional Prakrit salutation to all munis, widely shared across Śvetāmbara recitation. The lineage portion is the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipakṣa Gaccha) paṭṭāvalī as printed in the authoritative edition 'Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit'; its core verse-form names the succession down through Merutuṅgasūri and later teachers, with the recent ācāryas and the closing salutation given in classical Gujarati prose. No single named author is assigned to the assembled sūtra in the book; treat individual sub-verse authorship as uncertain.
structureArc: Two movements. (A) Salutation to munis — vv.1–2 (the 'Jāvanta Kevi Sāhū' gāthā): v.1 locates the munis across the two-and-a-half continents and oceans and the fifteen karma-bhūmis, describing them as bearers of the monastic emblems (rajoharaṇa, gucchā, paḍiggaha); v.2 praises them as holders of the five great vows and the eighteen-thousand śīlāṅga, of unbroken conduct, and bows to them all 'with head, heart, and mind.' (B) The paṭṭāvalī — the lineage portion (its own internal numbering (1)–(6) and (9)) opens by naming the first eight seats from Sudharmāsvāmī, Jambūsvāmī, Prabhavasvāmī, Sijjambhavasūri, Yaśobhadrasūri, Sambhūtivijayasūri, Bhadrabāhusvāmī to Sthūlibhadrasvāmī, then leaps 'paṭṭānupaṭṭe' (seat after seat) to the 47th seat-holder Āryarakṣitasūri, ornament of the Vidhipakṣa Gaccha. The verse blocks (1)–(6) then carry the chain forward through Jayasiṃha, Dharmaghoṣa, Mahendrasiṃha, Siṃhaprabha, Ajayasiṃha, Devendrasiṃha, Dharmaprabha, Siṃhatilaka, Mahendraprabha, Merutuṅga, Jayakīrti, Jayakeśarī, Siddhāntasāgara, Bhāvasāgara, Guṇanidhāna, Dharmamūrti, Kalyāṇasāgara, Amarasāgara, Vidyāsāgara, Udayasāgara, and Guṇamaṇi. The closing prose (9) names the recent Sāgara-suffixed ācāryas (Kīrtisāgara through Guṇodayasāgara), salutes the Vidhipakṣagaccha-nāyaka and the Achalagaccha-nāyaka, and ends by promising 108 three-fold (trikāla) salutations to Śrī Duḥṣpasahasūri, the ācārya tradition names as the last of this avasarpiṇī.
recurringImagery: Two motifs run across the sutra. (1) The unbroken seat (paṭṭa): the lineage is imagined as a single throne of teaching handed down 'paṭṭānupaṭṭe' — seat after seat — from Sudharmāsvāmī to the final ācārya, so that reverence to today's gaccha is reverence to one continuous transmission rather than to scattered individuals; each name is a successive holder of that one seat. (2) The cosmic span of renunciation: the opening gāthā gathers all monks everywhere — across the aḍhāī-dvīpa (two-and-a-half continents) and the fifteen karma-bhūmis — into a single act of homage, so the worshipper bows not to particular monks they have met but to the entire living body of true renunciants at once. The lineage movement then narrows that universal homage to the specific stream the worshipper belongs to, and the final 'last ācārya' salutation widens it again across all of remaining time.
keyThemes: the five great vows and eighteen-thousand śīlāṅga of a monk
the cosmic scope of renunciation across the karma-bhūmis
continuity of the teaching to the last ācārya of the age

glossary.definition: A Jain monk; a renunciant who has taken the great vows.
Reverential salutation or bowing; the act of paying homage.
The 'seat' of pontifical headship; one position in a teacher-to-disciple succession.
The ordered list of seat-holders (ācāryas) tracing an unbroken lineage.
'Seat after seat'; the phrase marking continuous succession from one ācārya to the next.
A monastic preceptor and head of an order; '-sūri' is the title suffixed to such names.
A monastic order or lineage-branch within Śvetāmbara Jainism.
The self-designation of the Anchalgachchha (Achalagaccha) in this book; literally the 'order of [correct] procedure.'
'Foremost of the age'; an epithet for a supremely authoritative teacher, applied here to Āryarakṣitasūri.
The 'two-and-a-half continents' — the region of the Jain universe where human life and thus monks can exist.
A 'land of action' where spiritual effort and liberation are possible; tradition counts fifteen such lands.
The five great vows of a monk: non-violence, truth, non-stealing, celibacy, non-possession.
The 'limbs of conduct'; the eighteen-thousand (aṭhāra-sahassa) combinations of monastic discipline.
The woolen broom a monk uses to gently sweep aside living beings; an emblem of ahiṃsā.
The alms-bowl carried by a monk.
Sacred self-study recitation; performed in the closing study-portion, not counted among the six Āvaśyakas.
Salutation offered at the three times (morning, noon, evening); a token of constant, lifelong reverence.

howToRead: A newcomer can simply hear this as two heartfelt bows — first to all true monks everywhere, then to the named chain of teachers that carries the tradition — and need not memorize the names. An advanced practitioner reads the paṭṭāvalī as a precise historical and devotional anchor, tracing their own gaccha 'seat after seat' from Sudharmāsvāmī through the 47th seat-holder Āryarakṣitasūri to the present gacchādhipati, and finally to Duḥṣpasaha, the prophesied last ācārya of the age.

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipakṣa edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim sutra text, ritual placement in Devasi, and the printed Anchalgachchha paṭṭāvalī

sources.title: Anchalgachchha (Achalgachchha) — overview and history
sources.consultedFor: Vidhipakṣa Gaccha identity and lineage context

sources.title: Āryarakṣitasūri / Anchalgachchha pattāvalī tradition
sources.consultedFor: Āryarakṣitasūri counted as the 47th paṭṭadhara from Sudharmāsvāmī

notes: The sutra's verse numbering in the source is composite: the opening 'Jāvanta Kevi Sāhū' gāthā is numbered (1)–(2), and the lineage portion restarts its own internal numbering (1)–(6) with the final prose block marked (9); the nine total verses reflect this layered structure, not a single continuous count. Individual sub-verse authorship is not stated in the book and is left uncertain. The claim that Duḥṣpasaha (Dushprasaha) is the last ācārya of the present descending half-cycle (avasarpiṇī) is standard Śvetāmbara tradition; exact spellings of the later Sāgara-suffixed ācāryas follow the book verbatim and may vary in other editions. The book spells some terms unconventionally (e.g. 'atiyār' for atichāra) and these readings are preserved rather than normalized. Source URLs are provided for general orientation only; the printed book is the golden source and no stable public URL for it was verified.
